Details:
Reverse osmosis (RO) has been demonstrated to be effective for the removal of alachlor (71 to 99 percent).  Multiple membrane materials have been shown to be effective, including thin film composites, nylon amide and cellulose acetate,  in single-pass RO units treating surface water. In a pilot-scale study, 100 percent removal was observed when ground water spiked with alachlor was treated using a thin film composite membrane [728].
Nanofiltration (NF) membranes were also able reject 100 percent of the influent alachlor at low pressures (50 to 200 psi).  The critical factor in determining the effectiveness of RO and NF for the removal of alachlor is the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the membrane relative to the molecular weight of alachlor (269.8).
